segment-text: Jason Reitman
from the novel by
FADE IN: TV LOCAL MORNING SHOW - CURRENT DAY A MORNING ANCHOR with a big smile addresses camera.
Okay, if you have a sweet tooth, you're not going to want to miss this one. They started out the most popular item on a menu of a little cafe in Syracuse, New York.
PHOTO: A Small Charming Cafe
PHOTO: A Pie Shop. B-CAM FOOTAGE of customers. The kitchen.
MORNING ANCHOR (CONT'D)
Oprah calls it her favorite pie. Hands. Down. We've got the man behind it all here with us. Henry Wheeler!
Reveal HENRY. He's 34 years old. Handsome and thoughtful.
Hi Donna, thanks for having me on.
I've heard that fights break out over these pies.
People have their favorites and they're not happy when we run out.
And I love the "A"... (notes the A shaped vent holes on the pie) Is that for someone special?
(smiles) My mom.
The morning anchor gushes for a heartbeat.

The real secret is to get back to the basics. Don't over handle the dough. Don't buy all those gadgets. Use an old fork to cut your mix. Use the heel of your hand to flatten the crust. We were actually built to make pies. Where on Earth did you learn to make such a perfect pie crust?
Henry smiles. Sighs. He takes a beat. A long beat.
I could never answer this question truthfully... Or at least completely.
EXT./INT. PIE SHOP - DAY
Henry enters, passing a line of waiting people. He waves to his staff and heads into the back.
INT. KITCHEN, PIE SHOP - DAY

Henry gives a glance to make sure everything is running fine, then finds his way to a work desk with his computer and mail. Rifles through some junk mail. Then, he opens a letter and suddenly stops cold.
INT. DARK ROOM - DAY
The back of a man's head. Peppered grey. We don't see his face. He's at a desk. Writing.
OPENING TITLES (perhaps over the streets of the neighborhood) EXT. ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
A classic American house amongst trees in full bloom. It's not the prettiest house on the street, but it could be. TITLE READS: "September 3rd, 1987"
INT. HENRY'S BEDROOM - MORNING
A PET HAMSTER (JOE) is fed by a twelve year old hand.
INT. KITCHEN, ADELE'S HOUSE - MORNING
The same 12 year old hand puts a kettle on, then moves to a cabinet with a broken hinge and grabs a tea packet.
INT. LIVING ROOM, ADELE'S HOUSE - MORNING
Same hand puts the record needle on a Joni Mitchell album.
INT. ADELE'S BEDROOM - MORNING
The MUG OF TEA is placed bedside by a sleeping woman. We hear the sheets rustle as she awakens.
Good morning.
INT. HENRY'S BEDROOM - MORNING

The same hand goes through a boy's closet. The clothes are worn and a little dated and a little youthful for twelve. We finally pull back to see Henry. He wears his sensitivity and the anxiety of pre-sexual enlightenment like wet pajamas. As he looks into his closet, his face reads dilemma. MOMENTS LATER, he is trying on a pair of pants for Adele. He clearly outgrew them a year ago. The cuffs are inches above his ankles. The buckle won't buckle. ADELE enters frame and acknowledges the moment with a sigh.
Okay. Okay. Pricemart, then.
EXT. DRIVEWAY, ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
Adele and Henry hop in a station wagon littered with leaves. It's clear that Adele doesn't take the car out often. All voice over is Henry as a 35 year old adult, present day.
My mother wasn't a shut in. She had just shut everyone out.
Adele drops the car into gear, but it slips back into NEUTRAL. Henry notices and pulls the stick into REVERSE.
INT. ADELE'S STATION WAGON - DAY
Adele drives. Her hands SHAKE slightly as they hold the wheel.
It was just the two of us after my
father left.
EXT. THE TEACUP RIDE, AMUSEMENT PARK - A DAY IN THE PAST

Henry with his FATHER'S NEW FAMILY. Wife, son, and kid sister pull the disc in the center of the teacup to spin faster. ADULT HENRY (V.O.) He said I should count the new baby he had with his new wife Marjorie,
Richard, Marjorie's son.
INT. HENRY'S BEDROOM, ADELE'S HOUSE - A DAY IN THE PAST
Adele is holding a photo taken of that teacup ride.
For the most part, my mother never mentioned my father, or the woman he was married to now.
(studying the photo) I would think your father would be worried about the way that baby's one eye doesn't match with the other. You'd think they'd have her tested. Does she seem different to you?
... Maybe a little.
That baby doesn't look anything like you.
I knew my part.
She cries... a lot.
EXT. HOLTON MILLS BRIDGE - DAY
Adele drives over the scenic bridge into town.
EXT. BANK PARKING LOT - DAY
Henry hops out of Adele's station wagon and enters the bank.
I'll be back in a sec.
INT. BANK - DAY
Henry walks up to the teller and hands her a check. We SEE that it's for CHILD SUPPORT.
Hey Henry. How's your mother? Fine.
Hope to see her in here one of these days.
The teller begins counting out twenty dollar bills.
EXT. PRICEMART PARKING LOT - DAY
Adele's station wagon pulls in and finds a spot. Inside the car, we see Adele take a deep breath.
I understood who my real family was. Her.
She looks over at Henry. Smiles.
INT. PRICEMART - DAY
It's obviously a holiday weekend. Festive supplies are in evidence. Families are stocking up for BBQs and get-togethers. Adele and Henry push a cart down the aisle.

Even if that meant only leaving the house once a month to pick up supplies at Pricemart. Henry notices a pregnant woman with a basket of baby toys approaching from afar. He takes control of the cart, pulling it right, to avoid the pregnant woman.
I think it's this way.
Adele follows.
INT. BOY'S CLOTHES DEPT, PRICEMART - DAY

MOMENTARY POV through scratched glass of Adele and Henry. We find Adele leafing through corduroys. Henry eyes the COMIC BOOK TURNSTILE twenty yards away. He watches his mom carefully for a moment, deciding whether she is okay.
Mom, I'm going to go check out the comic books.
Of course. Just don't wander far.
(already stepping away) Okay, I'll be right over there.
INT. MAGAZINE RACK, PRICEMART - DAY

Henry walks past the ADULT MAGAZINES, just slow enough to catch a glimpse of arms covering cleavage, moaning eyes. He slows down again at the women's magazines. COVER OF COSMO: What Women Wish Men Knew That They Don't. Henry reaches for one when he is bumped by a middle aged woman. Henry flinches and moves on to the COMICS RACK. He spins the turnstile of superheroes. We're following his gaze down past caped crusaders when we find a pair of MEN'S SHOES. They're worn in and muddy. Upon closer glance, one is stained with FRESH BLOOD, trailing from the man's ankle. Henry looks up following the legs and torso until he is staring up at FRANK. He is the illustration in a biology book, for a page they'd label Man.
Wonder if you could give me a hand here.
Frank is wearing a RED PRICEMART VEST. Henry notices blood over Frank's right ear. Trickling down from his hair line.
(careful) You're bleeding.
(like nothing) I fell out a window.
We should get help.
I wouldn't want to upset anyone. You came here with that woman over there, right?
Frank nods at Adele, who is just leaving Boys' Clothes Dept. My mom.
What I wanted to ask is, if you think she'd give me a ride.
We see Henry's gears turning. Frank takes an awkward step forward and places a firm grip on Henry's shoulder.
She looks like the type of person who would help me.
He was right. That's exactly the type my mom was.

Frank and Henry are walking with purpose in Adele's direction. Frank is trying not to limp. He grabs a BALLCAP, quickly yanks off the tag and puts it on his head. Henry notices but doesn't say a word. They turn a corner. Off comes the Red Pricemart Vest. Frank grabs a FLEECE and quickly throws it on.
INT. HARDWARE DEPT, PRICEMART - DAY
Adele is analyzing LIGHT BULBS. Frank and Henry walk up.
This is my mother Adele. Mom, this is Frank. He needs our help.
Adele looks at Frank, quickly studying his face. Then back to her son's face - Frank's muscular hand on his shoulder.
You have a good boy here, Adele. He was kind enough to offer me a ride.
Oh, well... um. He may have... We have a big afternoon. I don't think we can help you.
Frank reaches into his pocket and pulls out a baseball.
(to Henry) Shame. Thought we could have a catch.
I suck at baseball.
Maybe you used to. (fingering the stitches) Throw around the ball with your dad?
I only see him on Sundays...
(cuts Henry off) I'm sorry, but we really can't help you.
For a moment, Frank clenches his teeth in pain. Then, he MOVES HIS HAND to the back of Henry's neck. It's hard to interpret whether it's a gesture of kindness or a threat.
(to Adele) (Frankly), this needs to happen.
Adele looks deeply into Frank's eyes. She makes a decision.
INT. CHECKOUT, PRICEMART - DAY

Adele's items are rung up. A bomb shelter's worth of canned goods go by, beep, beep, beep, beep... Frank shows curiosity at the sheer amount. He then adds the baseball and mitt to the checkstand. Adele makes eye contact with the CHECKER. There's a moment where she could say something. She considers asking for help. She doesn't.
EXT. PARKING LOT, PRICEMART - DAY
Frank opens the rear passenger door of Adele's car and lays the fleece across the back seat.
Don't want to stain your upholstery. Thank you.
(to Henry) Why don't you ride in back with me.
(cautious) Okay.

Henry enters the rear passenger door and slides over. Frank sits down next to him and shuts the door. Adele rounds the car, trying not to panic. She enters the driver's side door and takes a seat. Shuts the door. Silence.
Where do you want to go?
Your house.
Adele suppresses a reaction.
Just for a moment to rest my legs.
She looks back and sees her son sitting next to this commanding and bloodied stranger. She turns the ignition.
INT. ADELE'S STATION WAGON - DAY

Driving back through the suburbs. Strangely calm and quiet. Just as they pull up to a stoplight, A POLICE CRUISER pulls up on their left. Henry notices. His heart quickens. Frank keeps his eyes straight, trying to act calm. Adele looks over at the cops. She thinks for a second, then puts on her signal and turns right, pulling away from the cruiser. They catch a glimpse of each other in the mirror. A moment.
Sounds like the rotors on your brakes might be a little thin. I'll take a look when we get back.
EXT. ADELE'S STREET - DAY

Adele's station wagon makes its way down the quiet cul-de-sac and finally parks in the driveway. Frank takes a second to look around for pedestrians before getting out to help Adele and Henry with the groceries. As they're emptying the car, Henry can't help but look back at his neglected home, seeing it through the eyes of a stranger for the first time in what's obviously a while. ADULT HENRY (V.O.) Other than religious types or
Adele opens the front door. Henry goes to hold it for her, but Frank already has an elbow on it. Smiles - Get inside.
INT. ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
Adele leads the grocery caravan into the kitchen. All the while, Henry looks around, seeing what Frank is seeing.
(quietly) You'll have to excuse the mess, we've been busy.
Henry glances at his mother confused.
Busy with what? The Spanish tapes?
We pan across POST IT NOTES with sharpie written Spanish words like Mesa, Silla, Agua, Basura... We can even hear... SPANISH LANGUAGE TAPE (V.O.)
Busy waiting to hear from Arak...
We pan along a faded photo of an African boy and an obviously mass produced letter from an Aid Organization.
Dear Henry, thank you for your letter...
Busy with the fox trot... The waltz...
We move along the DANCE FOOT PATTERNS stenciled on the floor. FLASH IMAGE: ADELE TEACHING HENRY TO FOX TROT IN THE KITCHEN She can really move. Henry follows, concentrating.
(into Henry's ear) A man should know how to dance... When a man can dance, the world is his oyster.
INT. ADELE'S KITCHEN - DAY
Frank places bags of groceries in front of a WALL OF CAMPBELL SOUP CANS. He eyes them suspiciously.
Nice place.
Adele positions herself near the KNIFE DRAWER.
(eyeing Frank cautiously) How do you take your coffee?
As is. Thank you, Adele.

Frank notices all the window sheers have been drawn. Thinks on this for a second. He moves to the kitchen window and looks out back - The backyard is an unkept mess of overgrown grass leading to a thick woods. We hear the distant SOUND OF A TRAIN'S WHISTLE. He takes in the kitchen table - a foreign object. There's a silence. One that acknowledges that neither Adele or Frank know how they've made it this far.
How did you hurt your leg?
Frank weighs the question.

I'm going to be straight with you here, Henry. (looks to Adele making coffee) I'm not going to lie. (back to Henry) I hurt my leg - my leg, and my head - jumping out a second floor window at a hospital they'd taken me to get my appendix out.
INT. RECOVERY ROOM, HOSPITAL - DAY

Frank lying in bed attached to an IV. A nurse sits at his bedside, preparing an IV. The guard outside the door is turned away. The look on the nurse's face changes. She knows what is about to happen. In one move, Frank leaps out of the hospital bed, pulls the nurse aside, yanks the IV from his wrist. We whip around to catch Frank grab the top of the window sill and hurtle out feet first.
INT. ADELE'S KITCHEN - DAY
Frank continues.
... At the prison. That's how I got out.
Adele examines Frank.
This morning.
Adele opens a drawer to grab a spoon. She slips a knife into her pocket as she uses the spoon to add sugar to the coffee.
(fear and awe) You escaped? So now the police are looking for you.
I would have gotten further except for the damn leg.
EXT. HOSPITAL - DAY
A wide enough shot to see a REAL DANGEROUS FALL. Frank lands on the ground. His foot hits an uneven piece of lawn twisting his ankle painfully. SNAP... goes a ligament.
INT. ADELE'S KITCHEN - DAY
Adele hands Frank his coffee. She seems to be taking in every moment. Every inch of Frank's face. Every gesture.
I'd be grateful if you let me stay til nightfall. I'd try to help out. Wouldn't ask anything of you.
(cautious) You just did.
Nothing more than a place to lay
low and rest my leg for a few
How do I know you won't hurt us?
I've never intentionally hurt anyone in my life.
Adele considers this. We want to believe him. Henry observes his mother carefully.
You understand how that could be hard to believe.
I do. How far are the train tracks from your backyard? Running?
Limping.
FRANK
Twenty minutes.
People come over. Often. Randomly. Friends, salesmen, religious people...
Frank hides his disbelief.
I'll make a run for the train at nightfall. I can stay out of the way...
I mean it would be noticed if I went quiet. Even for a little bit.
I just escaped prison. Last place I want to go is back.
(quickly) I'm stronger than you think.
(quicker) I don't doubt it.
And I won't let anything happen to my son.
Frank understands the importance of this moment.
He's in good hands.
Adele looks at FRANK'S HANDS.
EXT. HIGH DRIVE, FRANK'S GRANDMOTHER'S FARM - LATE 60'S
FRANK'S HANDS at 18 years old. He's LOADING HAY. His hands are strong, assured, practiced at what he's doing.
Frank had good hands. This was
Frank looks up and catches eyes with MANDY, also 18. She's being toured by Frank's grandmother. They share a smile.
INT. KITCHEN - A NIGHT IN THE 70'S
Adele and Gerald dance. His hand firmly grasps her back. They are capable of that rare ballroom dancing that looks effortless and improvised.
So did my father. He knew how to move a woman around the dance floor. That meant a lot in my mom's book.
(always teaching) Some men just set their hand on your shoulder or against your back.
INT. LIVING ROOM, ADELE'S HOUSE - A DAY IN 1987
Adele is teaching Henry to dance.
There has to be strong pressure there...
EXT. FRANK'S GRANDMOTHER'S FARM - LATE AFTERNOON
Mandy works a fruit stand on the road. Frank drops off a few pies to sell. Mandy dips her finger in one of them and tastes it. Frank takes in her dangerous smile.
EXT. FRANK'S GRANDMOTHER'S FARM - TWILIGHT
18 year old Frank and Mandy are chasing each other through a field of tall grass towards the farm house. He grabs her.
... Something to push back against.
INT. KITCHEN, ADELE'S HOUSE - A DAY IN THE PAST
Adele opening a sad can of food. Henry hides his disappointment in dinner.
Your father was very handsome. Same as you'll be. (a sweet discarded thought) If we could have just danced all the time.
INT. INSURANCE OFFICE - DAY
Gerald speaks to a couple from behind a desk.
Instead my father got a job selling insurance policies.
INT. KITCHEN, ADELE'S HOUSE - A DAY IN THE PAST
Adele curled up with a book. Henry watches from the floor.
I don't think losing my father broke my mother's heart, but rather losing love itself...
EXT. BARN, FRANK'S GRANDMOTHER'S FARM - DAY
18 year old Frank leads Mandy into the barn from the high drive. They eye each other in the dim shafts of light.
... The look of someone who thinks you're beautiful every day.
INT. ADELE'S BEDROOM - A DAY IN THE PAST
Henry brings Adele breakfast in bed. On the tray is a birthday gift - A homemade coupon book.
Even at that age I understood this and wanted to make up for some of the neglect. I made her a coupon book that said "Husband for a day". I cleaned...
HENRY SCRUBS THE SLATS BETWEEN THE FORKS AND KNIVES
... did little chores around the house.
HENRY TIGHTENS THE HINGE ON A DOOR
There was a coupon for a shoulder rub...
HENRY ATTEMPTS TO MASSAGE HIS MOTHER'S SHOULDERS WITH HIS SMALL TWELVE YOUR OLD HANDS
... and a bubble bath. HENRY POURS LIQUID SOAP INTO A RUNNING BATH
EXT. MOVIE THEATER - A NIGHT IN THE PAST
Henry walks his mother into the theater.
I even took her on a date.
INT. HENRY'S BEDROOM - NIGHT
Henry lies awake, keenly aware of the wall behind him.
At the time I was too young to understand the part of being Husband for a Day I was not equipped to carry out, but I sensed my own terrible inadequacy...
INT. UPSTAIRS CORRIDOR - NIGHT
Henry peeks into Adele's room to see his mother sleeping, with a pillow between her legs.
I could feel her loneliness and longing before I had a name for it.
INT. GERALD'S KITCHEN - SOME TIME LATER
Henry is having a late night bowl of cereal with his dad.
Mom's fine. She just gets sad sometimes.
Gerald weighs the information.
You could come live with Marjorie and me. And Chloe and Richard. (a beat) (MORE)
I mean, if that was something you wanted, we could have her evaluated.
Mom's great. She's learning to play the cello.
I just ask because I get the idea her depression is getting worse.
A moment. Henry could reveal more, but he can tell his father doesn't actually want him to move in. He's just trying to do/say the right thing.
Mom takes care of me great. We do fun things all the time. Friends come over. We have hobbies. (adding) We're learning Spanish.
Gerald smiles and nods. Glad that's taken care of.
INT. KITCHEN, ADELE'S HOUSE - A NIGHT IN THE PAST
Adele opens the fridge, removes a JUG OF MILK, and begins to slowly pour the contents on the floor as if she were watering a pot of flowers.
He was right, but I knew better than to say. He just wanted to hear that I was okay.
ADELE'S TELEVISION - LATE AFTERNOON A NEWS REPORT on Frank's escape. Footage of a ROAD BLOCK.
ANCHOR WOMAN Commuters may have experienced delays this afternoon on their commute home Roadblocks on route Seventy Three. Who are the police looking for? Frank's MUGSHOT appears on screen.
Frank Chambers, an inmate of Stinchfield prison, who escaped earlier this morning.
INT. LIVING ROOM, ADELE'S HOUSE
Adele, Frank, and Henry watching the report. Adele and Henry sit on the sofa, her arms around him. Frank stands. ON TV: The head of highway patrol is interviewed.
Considering his injuries, we believe he hasn't gotten far. Keep in mind that he is dangerous and quite possibly armed.
Henry looks to Frank - Is he armed? ON TV: Shot of the hospital that zooms in on a broken window. ANCHOR WOMAN Chambers was recovering from
ON TV: Interview with the nurse.
He whipped me around and next thing I know, he was out the window.
Authorities would like to remind
our viewers that this kind of leap
Henry and Adele examine Frank's face... Is it the face of a killer? Frank doesn't betray his calm demeanor.
It didn't happen that way.
Adele and Henry are too nervous to push Frank on this point.
Isn't it against the law to hide a fugitive?
You have a wise son here, Adele. Good to know he's looking out for you.

(trying to calm her son as well as Frank) It's all going to be fine. It would only be a problem if someone found Frank here... And that doesn't need to happen. He can just lay low for a few hours and then keep moving.

True enough. But it's still not acceptable to place you and your family here in jeopardy. If someone were to come by, it would need to look like I kidnapped you. (a beat) That's why I'm going to tie you up.
Henry looks to his mother. Adele's breath quickens.
(to Adele) It's about keeping up appearances. I think you understand what I mean.
(careful) Who are you...?
Only you. Henry here knows he doesn't want anything to happen to his mother. Right, Henry?
Henry eyes Frank cautiously.
INT. BASEMENT, ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY

Adele leads Frank downstairs. It's cluttered. Some nursery items visible. She pulls out a box that contains a roll of braided cord. Frank checks the strength. We can tell Adele's heart is beating faster. She reaches in her pocket for the knife from the kitchen. We can see through the fabric of her dress that she has it in her grasp, when...
This'll work.
He grabs the rope. Adele releases the knife and pulls her hands from her pockets.
INT. KITCHEN, ADELE'S HOUSE - SAME
Meanwhile, Henry has been left alone in the house. ADULT HENRY (V.O.) Across the street was Mr. Jervis's house. After that, the Farnsworths
steps to the front door and a good
INT. DINING ROOM, ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY

Frank checks a wooden chair for splinters before slowly lowering Adele to a seat. He kneels down at each of her feet and begins tying them tenderly to the legs of the chair. Adele attempts a comforting look to Henry. Frank notices.
(to Henry) I don't want any of this to upset you, son. There are just some things a person has to do in these types of situations. (then) Why don't you go grab one of your comic books.
Henry takes a beat and looks to his mother. She gives a little nod and a reassuring smile. Henry reticently walks away as Frank picks up Adele's calf, admiring her foot. Beauty.
FRANK (CONT'D)

Frank takes Adele's thin pale wrists, first her left, then her right, angling her elbows gently, as he draws them behind the chair's back and ties them together. It's dead silent. Quiet enough to hear the rough chord pull into a tight little knot. Frank steps in front of Adele and their eyes meet. Henry has returned with a comic book, sitting in the next room, watching his mother stare into Frank's eyes. Frank looks over at Henry in the dining room, measuring the boy's fear at the sight of his mother tied up.
You got a pantry?
INT. STAIRWELL, ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
Frank opens the basement door to find shelves of canned foods. He takes in the inventory. Looks back at Henry.
How'd you like to try the best chili you've ever eaten?
INT. BASEMENT, ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
Frank opens a freezer and removes a block of ground beef that's covered in frost.
INT. FRANK'S GRANDMOTHER'S KITCHEN - LATE 60'S
Young Frank being taught to make chili by his grandmother. her hands over his hands.
It was his grandmother's recipe. He'd grown up on her farm and learned that along with everything else important from her.
INT. KITCHEN, ADELE'S HOUSE - AFTERNOON

Henry watches carefully as Frank makes quick moves at the stove. He knows his way around a kitchen. For a moment Henry looks at his mother's hands and feet. He thinks about untying the knots, but just then Frank looks back.
Grab a bowl.
INT. LIVING ROOM, ADELE'S HOUSE - AFTERNOON

Henry takes his chili back into the living room and settles in front of the TV - RED SOX GAME. Takes a bite. It's delicious. ADULT HENRY (V.O.) And he was right about the chili. Frank sits down next to Adele at the kitchen table. He sets a bowl in front of her and waves at it with his hand to cool. Then brings a spoon to Adele's lips.
There was something about the way
Adele swallows the bite.
You might think it was humiliating, to sit there like that. If he put too much on the spoon, or too little, she'd simply have to take it.
Lifts the spoon. Blows on it. Brings it to Adele's lips.
But every spoonful, he made sure was the right amount.
I don't imagine your father has told you anything about sex?
EXT. FRONT PORCH - A DAY IN THE PAST
Adele and Henry sit on the HAMMOCK.
Most people put all this focus onto the physical changes you'll be going through soon.
It's okay, they explained everything in our health assembly.
There is another aspect that your health teacher is unlikely to explore. People act as if making love is all about secretions and body functions.
Mom, it's okay.
For all the discussions of body parts and hormones, they forget to mention how it feels.
EXT. RURAL ROAD - SUNDOWN
Frank's truck drives along. Mandy rides next to him. ADELE (V.O.) There is another kind of hunger.
EXT. TOP OF A HILL - MAGIC HOUR
The truck is parked. Frank lays out a blanket in the bed. Mandy smiles and lies down. Frank climbs on top of her.
Keep your belt on cowboy.
A hunger for human touch.
INT. ADELE'S KITCHEN - NIGHT
Frank takes a napkin and dabs the corner of Adele's mouth.
Desire. People never tell you about how it feels. The longing.
A flood light washes across the windows. We see a cruiser through the sheers pass by. Frank takes note.
What time do the trains start in the morning?
I don't know.
Just have to keep an ear out for 'em I guess.
INT. HENRY'S BEDROOM - NIGHT
Henry lays awake, staring at the ceiling. Listening. His curiosity overcomes him. He tiptoes out his doorway.
INT. UPSTAIRS CORRIDOR - NIGHT

Henry crawls up to his mother's bedroom doorway to find her asleep - her wrist tied to the bedpost. He then crawls his way to the stairs until he has an angle on the living room. He can see Frank's boots resting on the arm of the sofa. Henry inches forward until he can sees Frank's legs... Frank's torso... Frank's neck... and then Frank's face, hidden in the shadows. It takes a moment for our vision to adjust. We adjust the exposure until we can JUST see Frank's open eyes... staring back at us. Henry's breath quickens. Frank nods. Henry attempts to calm himself before tip toeing back to bed.
INT. HENRY'S BEDROOM - NEXT MORNING
Henry gets out of bed. Was last night a dream? He walks over to his HAMSTER CAGE and feeds his pet, JOE. Then steps out.
INT. TOP OF STAIRS - SAME

Henry looks into his mother's room. Adele is sleeping. One wrist in evidence, still restrained to the bed post. At the bottom of the stairs, he looks into the living room. Evidence of Frank having slept the night on the couch.
INT. KITCHEN, ADELE'S HOUSE - SAME

Henry looks around, but Frank is nowhere to be seen. The back door is open. The sound of the cicadas whispers in. Henry wonders for a moment... Is that it? Is he gone? We can tell he kind of misses him when... Frank emerges from the basement.
Couldn't find any eggs.

Henry whips around as Frank steps over to the oven and opens it. He's made a makeshift splint out of a wooden spoon and a dish rag. Frank strains as he removes a tray of biscuits from the oven, his appendix scar staining his shirt.
We don't stock up much on groceries. Mostly canned goods.
Henry walks passed the chair that Adele occupied the night before, rope still tied to the legs.
You've got enough room in back for chickens. Three or four nice little Rhode Island Reds.
EXT. FRANK'S GRANDMOTHER'S FARM - 1970'S MORNING
Frank's and Mandy's legs walking through pecking chickens as they drop feed.
Keep you in fresh eggs. Poultry.
INT. KITCHEN, ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
Henry goes to cut open the biscuit with a knife... Frank quickly grabs the knife from his hand. For a moment, it seems like a defensive move, but instead...
(appalled) Never slice a biscuit. (tearing one open with his hands) You want to pull them apart, so you get all the surfaces.
FLASH: FRANK'S GRANDMOTHER TEARING OPEN A BISCUIT FRANK & FRANK'S GRANDMOTHER ... Peaks and valleys...
INT. KITCHEN, ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
... More places for the butter to soak in.
There is something undeniably fatherly about this moment that is foreign to Henry.
We don't usually keep butter around. We use margarine.
Now that's a crime.
INT. DINING ROOM - DAY
Frank notices Henry trying to eye the front of the NEWSPAPER. It features the prison photo of Frank from TV.
I don't blame you for wondering. Just know, there's more to this story than you'll see in that paper there.
Henry stops looking.
You got brothers or sisters?
No, um, my mom tried but...
Frank takes this in without reacting.
I've got a stepbrother and a halfsister. My dad takes me out to dinner with them on Sundays.
How does a fellow let a woman like your mother get away?
He got together with his secretary.
Huh. Good riddance.
Henry works up the confidence to ask a question.
How did you escape?
I told the guard at the hospital if he left, I was going to jump out
the window. He heard that and went
Good morning.
Frank and Henry turn to see Adele enter the room.
What are those?
Biscuits. They're amazing.
Henry's joy for his breakfast makes Adele cautious. Adele offers her wrists to Frank for binding.
I don't think we'll be needing that any longer. But if the day comes when you have to say I tied you up, you won't be lying. (another thought) Haven't heard any trains go by.
Holiday weekend. Might be a funny schedule.
(offering a biscuit) Breakfast?
She wants to resist the invitation.
You have to try them.
Adele submits to the biscuit. Tries to suppress a smile.
INT. LIVING ROOM, ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY

Henry puts on one of his mom's folk records. There's a warp in the record from sun damage. We can even see the beam of sunlight that must cut across the record player at times. Music continues through the following short scenes.
INT. KITCHEN, ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
Frank brushes his teeth using a dish towel.
INT. GARAGE - DAY
Frank rolls the car into the garage. Later, we find Frank under Adele's car checking the breaks. Henry sits by, watching with curiosity.
Hey, Hank. Grab me the 3/4 socket wrench.
Henry looks into the tool box. He doesn't know a socket wrench from a screwdriver.
After a beat Frank slides out. Henry sits there, a bit embarrassed. Frank understands immediately. He points to the side of the wrench that's closed.
Socket wrench.

Frank disappears under the car. Music comes up as we begin a quiet montage of Frank doing chores around the house. Henry is by his side for everything. ADULT HENRY (V.O.) That day, Frank changed the oil in the car and replaced a fuse for the blinkers. Then he cleaned the filters on the furnace.
EXT. SIDE OF ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
Frank examines the wood pile with Adele. ADULT HENRY (V.O.) He checked out the cord of firewood
FRANK WAXING THE FLOOR WHILE HENRY SITS ON THE COUNTER
Then he washed the floor and waxed it.
EXT. BACKYARD - DAY
Frank stands over Henry's shoulder, placing the BASEBALL in his small twelve year old hand. Adele watches from a window.
Before you can throw a ball, you need to know how to hold a ball... How to feel the stitches.
Frank turns the ball in Henry's hand till his fingers line up with the red stitches.
You feel that?
INT. KITCHEN, ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
Frank sits at the kitchen table, his twisted ankle up on another chair. Adele is hemming the ripped pant cuff, being careful not to poke Frank with the needle.
(to Adele) You bite your lip when you sew. I'm trying not to stick you.
(re: a scar on her knee) How'd you get this? Stars and Stripes Forever routine
at my dance recital. Halfway
through rehearsal, they got rid of the ramp. I tapped myself right off
stage.

Frank touches the scar tenderly. Henry watches his mother carefully. Everyone seems to have forgotten... KNOCK, KNOCK, KNOCK! - A banging at the kitchen door that leads to the side of the house. We see a SILHOUETTE of a man in the window of the split kitchen door. All three stop. We can almost see the vein in Frank's neck raise. His eyes go dead serious. Henry looks - Frank is holding Adele's wrist. Her hands shake.
Answer the door, Henry. Let'em know your mother's out.
Frank pulls Henry in for a second.
(quietly serious) I know I can trust you not to get nervous and say something you'd regret.

Henry nods. The day's activities suddenly erased. KNOCK, KNOCK, KNOCK! Henry walks over to the kitchen door as Frank and Adele curl behind a corner where they can't be seen. Perhaps Frank stands just out of sight, his hand holding the back of Henry's shirt as a reminder of his presence. Henry opens the top half of the kitchen door, revealing his neighbor MR. JERVIS. A friendly man in his 50's carrying a BUCKET OF PEACHES. It's clear that he has a thing for Adele.
Oh hi Henry, I was looking for your mother.
I'm sorry Mr. Jervis. She's not home right now.
Mr. Jervis gives a disappointed look. Adele is always home.
Oh... Well, that's not like her... Has she been following the news? She leave you here by yourself?
I just mean she can't come to the door... She's taking a bath. Aaah...
(re: the peaches) Are those for her?
Yeah, got so many of these, didn't know what to do. Thought your mother might like them.
Jervis hands Henry the peaches.
Thank you. I'm sure she'll appreciate them...
(not letting up) Sure she's not around? There's something I'd like to discuss with her.
Can I give her a message?

(hesitates) Look, Henry, there's some... I'm not sure if you heard, but there's a guy loose - just broke out of Stinchfield. He's probably long gone by now, but until they find him, I just wouldn't be opening the door without an adult.
Henry measures his response.
Thank you for telling me. (a thought) What did he do?
Some pretty awful things, son. (thinks) He... What?
(immediately regrets telling Henry) Look, I doubt he's anywhere near here by now... Just remember, I'm right across the street. You have any problems, just give me a jingle.
Mr. Jervis exits and Henry closes the door. Adele and Frank enter the room and size up the peaches.
(sets down the peaches) He had more than he needed.
Frank senses Henry's apprehension.
(pats him on the shoulder) You did good, Hank.
Henry smiles proudly. Adele shows concern.
Henry, why don't you go check on Joe.
I'm sure he's fine.
It's a hot day. Make sure his water hasn't dried up.
Henry leaves. Once he's gone, Adele summons up some courage and confronts Frank.
You can't do that again.
What did I...?
My son is not your look out.
A beat as Frank and Adele stare each other down.
He's not your accomplice and I don't want him doing that again.
Frank tightens up. Does she remember who I am?
INT. HENRY'S BEDROOM - SAME
Henry takes a little feeder and brings it to the tiny mouth of his pet hamster Joe. He can still hear the warble of Frank and Adele talking downstairs.
INT. KITCHEN, ADELE'S HOUSE - SAME
Now Adele, it was just a neighbor.
It doesn't matter.
Don't forget what's going on here.
Not for a second. (however) There hasn't been a man in this house for a while now. I see how he looks at
you. What he wants. That encouragement a pat on his shoulder so badly. And I want it for him. But not for that.
I can't promise what's going to show up at the door.
(believe me) I know.
A beat.
Okay, then.
A look between the two of them resolves the conversation. Frank grabs a peach, takes a bite.
Good peach. Too bad they're all so ripe. Never get through 'em all.
We should throw away most of em before they rot.
I have another idea.

(note: we will show Frank in the moment with Henry & Adele and crosscut with Frank & Mandy as teenagers) Frank, Adele, and Henry stand at the kitchen table peeling the peaches and chopping them into pieces.
This recipe was taught to me by the wisest woman I ever knew.
FRANK'S GRANDMOTHER CHOPPING PEACHES WITH TEENAGE FRANK Adele and Henry's pile of chopped peaches builds.
Not so even. We want variety.
INT. ADELE'S KITCHEN - DAY
Frank goes through Adele's pantry, pulling ingredients. TEENAGE FRANK AND MANDY GRABBING INGREDIENTS
INT. ADELE'S KITCHEN - DAY
A PIE DISH is set out.

Filling is easy. What I want to talk about is crust. We need to keep our ingredients cool. Hot day like this is challenge enough. We need to move fast. Phone rings when you're making a crust, you just let
them call back. Frank scoops three cups of FLOUR into a bowl. Follows it with a pinch of SALT.
How much salt was that?
(holds his finger and thumb apart) Bout that much... (smiles) Pie crust is a forgiving thing. You can make all kinds of mistakes, but don't forget the salt.
Frank opens the fridge. Nothing in the butter tray.
That's right. No butter.
Mrs. Farnsworth probably has some.
Henry looks to Frank.
What are you waiting for? Listen to your mother. Times a ticking.
Henry runs out.
EXT. ADELE'S STREET - DAY

Henry runs to the Farnsworth house. We dolly with him, backyard to backyard, giving us a nice view of the neighborhood and its seclusion. Mrs. Farnsworth answers the door. MRS. FARNSWORTH Oh, hi Henry. How can I help you?
Can we borrow a stick of butter?
Is your mom baking?
We're making pie.
Well, okay... I'll be right back.
INT. ADELE'S KITCHEN
Henry slaps down the stick of BUTTER next to a can of CRISCO.

Hank, before you sits one of the great dilemmas of life: Sure butter has superior flavor, but nothing beats lard for flakiness. All your life you'll meet people who will try to convince you of one over the other. What do you think we do?
Henry thinks hard.
(smiles) Smart boy, Adele.

Crisco and butter go into the bowl with the flour. Frank holds Adele's hand, Adele holds Henry's hand, and Henry holds the fork as they cut the butter/lard into the flour. FLASH IMAGE: TEENAGE FRANK AND MANDY CUTTING THE MIX

It's all about instinct. Pay too much attention to recipes and you forget how to feel. It's like reading a book on gardening when all you got to do is get dirt underneath your fingernails. It's like dancing, right Adele?
Frank and Adele share a smile. Frank pulls a pitcher of water from the fridge and begins to sparingly add it to the contents of the bowl.
Most people drown their mix. Sure, they get a perfect looking ball of dough but nobody's giving prizes for that. Might as well be eating cardboard.
FLASH IMAGE: MANDY THROWS A PIECE OF DOUGH AT FRANK Frank takes Adele's hands.
(quietly to Adele) Get your hands in there, but don't over handle. There's heat in your hands - Keep it loose - That's it... Should be right on the verge of crumbling at any moment.

Henry mixes it with his hands before Frank grabs the dough and drops it on a piece of wax paper. TEENAGE FRANK TAKES HIS GRANDMOTHER'S ROLLING PIN Adult Frank takes a nearby WINE BOTTLE and uses it as a rolling pin flattening out the dough. Close up on the texture. Cracks and holes like the desert floor.
Oh this is looking real good. Patch up those holes now.
Frank and Henry grab extra pieces of dough and fill the holes. Frank uses his palm to flatten the extra pieces. Henry mirrors his every move.
Heel of the hand.
FRANK'S GRANDMOTHER SHOWS YOUNG FRANK THE HEEL OF HIS HAND TEENAGE FRANK SHOWS MANDY THE HEEL OF HER HAND ADULT FRANK SHOWS ADELE THE HEEL OF HER HAND
People buy all these fancy gadgets. Sometimes the best tool is right there attached to your own body. The pie plate is placed upside down on the dough. Then, using
everyone's hands, the plate is flipped over with the dough inside. Frank peels off the wax paper. Beauty.
FRANK (CONT'D)
Frank shakes some Tapioca along the inside.
Gram's secret ingredient. Scatter little Tapioca like salt on an icy road. Cure for a soggy crust. (to Henry) Alright, fill'er up Hank.

Henry starts filling the pie with peaches. MANDY EATS A SLICE OF PEACH. TEEN FRANK PLAY SLAPS HER HAND Adele and Frank flatten out another piece of dough to cover the pie. Frank demonstrates how to use the heel of her hand.
(looks over to Henry) Just a little more sugar. Not too sweet.

Frank sprinkles a little cinnamon over the chopped peaches. And after a good minute of commotion, everything now stops. Frank is holding a WAX PAPER SHEET with the top layer of dough. It needs to be flipped on top of the pie, but they've made it so flaky that it looks as though it could go everywhere.
Moment of truth. Can't hesitate.
MANDY HOLDING THE WAX PAPER SHEET COVERED IN DOUGH. UNSURE.
Can't go too fast. Calls for a steady hand... and a steady heart. I think you can handle this Adele.
Adele looks down at the pie.
I don't know when my mom's hands
started shaking exactly. ADELE'S TREMBLING HANDS Applying lipstick. Threading a needle. Holding her cello.
INT. KITCHEN, ADELE'S HOUSE - SAME

Adele takes the TOP CRUST in her hands. She holds her breath. She looks to Frank. He smiles with confidence. FRANK'S GRANDMOTHER QUICKLY FLIPS HER WAXPAPER SHEET MANDY QUICKLY FLIPS HER WAXPAPER SHEET WITH A SHRIEK Adele hesitates. Her hands shake. With one hand, Frank steadies her wrist.
Help me put a roof on this house. (quietly) You can do this. I know you can.
Adele takes a breath, then quickly flips the crust on top of the peaches. It's perfect. Her face immediately warms. Perfect.
FRANK (CONT'D)

All three pinch the ends to finish off the pie. A few fork holes in the top, a brush of milk, and a sprinkle of sugar dusts it to completion. Adele places it in the oven. MANDY SHUTS THE OVEN AND KISSES TEENAGE FRANK Adele shuts her oven and smiles. How long?
FRANK'S GRANDMOTHER GIVES A WISE LOOK TO CAMERA FRANK'S GRANDMOTHER Bake her till she smells like pie. A beat as the joy of pie making settles over the three of them.
(to Henry) Even the richest man in America isn't eating tastier pie than we are tonight.
Adele is holding the pairing knife. After considering whether she should put it in her pocket, she puts it back in the drawer.
INT. LIVING ROOM, ADELE'S HOUSE - LATER THAT EVENING

Adele and Frank are sharing a corner of the couch, eating their slices of pie. There is an intimacy between them that Adele doesn't dare acknowledge. Henry is on the floor playing a handheld PacMan. His empty pie plate beside him. With her son's back turned, Adele slightly leans back into Frank, exposing her neck an inch. Frank lowers his chin. Adele closes her eyes.
EXT. BUS STOP - DAY
Teenage Frank, in military dress, kissing Mandy's neck.
INT. BUS - DAY
Teenage Frank looking out the window. He begins to pull away. Mandy has already turned.
INT. ADELE'S LIVING ROOM - NIGHT

Frank's lips touch Adele's neck silently. HENRY'S POV - The screen of his PACMAN reflecting Frank and Adele on the sofa. Just as we hear the quietest kiss... A wash of light cuts across the window. Frank walks over and takes a look outside. FRANK'S POV - A POLICE CRUISE patrols the street, using a side lamp to see into the shadows.
I should go. They won't be back for a few hours. It's as good a window as I'm going to get.
Adele and Henry look up. It's clear they want him to stay.
Are you sure?
I'm not just thinking 'bout myself.
Maybe tomorrow once your stitches have had a little more time.
They won't stop looking, Adele. Henry watches this negotiation with rapt attention.
They have dogs that can smell your blood. You should probably let your stitches heal.
Frank looks at Henry. Really? Henrys nods.
This was a complete fabrication.
You should stay.
INT. UPSTAIRS CORRIDOR, ADELE'S HOUSE - NIGHT
HENRY'S POV of the downstairs sofa. It's empty. We turn to Adele's door. Light shines from underneath. Perhaps we hear Frank playing Adele's cello like a bass.
INT. HENRY'S BEDROOM, ADELE'S HOUSE - NIGHT

Henry is holding his hamster, Joe. His fellow prisoner. He says good night to his pet and moves to his bed. The room goes dead quiet. We hear only the sounds coming from Adele's room. The adjustments of two people in bed together for the first time. A preamble of noises, that are not easily discernible... Henry tries to interpret what sounds like... Footsteps... short thuds... A sheet untucked... A hand grabbing a bed frame... A bed spring... We slowly descend over Henry, holding his baseball, well aware of the wall that separates us from Adele's room. The bed noises continue. Not in a profane way. Rather a new couple finding their rhythm. ADULT HENRY (V.O.)
quietly dissipate. I thought about
INT. CLASSROOM - A DAY IN THE PAST
Pushing in on Henry at his desk, staring forward. ADULT HENRY (V.O.)
the first time. The stiffening. We reach Henry and turn to see the object of his gaze: RACHEL MCCANN'S BRASTRAP, barely peeking out from her collar.
Showing up like a drunk crashing a party. Never leaving when told.

WE CATCH GLIMPSES of Henry's gaze: RACHEL'S THIGH as she stands and her skirt rises. The line of her BRA CLASP through the back of her shirt. The ELASTIC BAND of her underwear riding a millimeter above her waistline.
These were the images I returned to at night...
INT. HENRY'S BEDROOM - NIGHT
Henry lost in thought.
... particularly now that the evening was no longer silent.
INT. LIVING ROOM, ADELE'S HOUSE - NEXT MORNING

Henry enters and walks by the sofa where Frank's pillow and blanket remain folded. It is clear he slept with Adele the night before. He walks to the record player and puts on one of his mother's folk albums. Leonard Cohen? Karen Dalton? This music accompanies us as we see Frank working on the house.
INT. STAIRWELL, ADELE'S HOUSE - MORNING
Frank hammers down a loose floorboard. ADULT HENRY (V.O.) The next day, Frank fixed the loose floorboard on the stairs.
INT. KITCHEN, ADELE'S HOUSE - MORNING
Frank sprays the hinges on the kitchen door. Adele hands Frank a mug of coffee. ADULT HENRY (V.O.) He got the squeak out of the kitchen door.
EXT. ADELE'S BACKYARD - DAY
Frank finds a patch of tall grass. Adele and Henry help him flatten it with their feet in a four foot square.
Cold weather's coming, but with enough straw, chickens winter over just fine. All they need's a warm body to huddle up to in the night.
INT. GARAGE - DAY
The car is up on a jack. Henry rolls the spare tire over. Frank is loosens the lug nuts.
I'll tell you now, you don't want to wait to learn how to fix it. Not if you've got some lady in the seat beside you when it happens.
Together they pull the rear tire off and set it down. FRANK FOLDING LAUNDRY WITH ADELE
He did laundry. He ironed. And in return my mom taught him to Rumba.
ADELE WALKING FRANK THROUGH A FEW MOVES.
One, two, three... bring your foot back, four, five, six... that's it.
Frank is getting it.
INT. LIVING ROOM, ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
Over Frank's shoulder, we look out the window to see Henry bike away.
For lunch, Frank thought it only right that we fire up the barbecue. So he sent me to pick up groceries.
EXT. ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
In a shot we will repeat later, Henry comes biking down the front lawn and continuing down the street.
EXT. SUBURBS - DAY
Henry bikes past people celebrating the long weekend. Kids play in sprinklers. Men scrape off BBQs.
INT. CORNER GROCERY - DAY

Henry is filling a shopping cart. He's got a grocery list. Again, we see shoppers dressed for the holiday, preparing for holiday cook outs. Henry grabs a package of ground beef from the butcher. Then passes a familiar BUDWEISER AD of women staring back.
BEVERAGE AISLE
Henry walks past a fourteen year old girl with braided hair and a bulky sweater over toothpick legs - ELEANOR. Henry grabs a SIX PACK of Coke.
You know drinking from aluminum gives you Alzheimer's.
Henry tries to hide the thrill of being spoken to by a strange girl. There's something dark and unusual about her.
Is that true?
Uh huh. It's the perfect crime cause you never remember how you got it.
Henry doesn't know how to respond.
Is there anything to do in this town?
There's bowling?
She isn't impressed. Before Henry can think of something...
Eleanor!... Get over here...

Eleanor waves goodbye then wisps around the corner giving Henry a chance to gain his breath. CHECK STAND Henry is placing fresh ingredients on the conveyer belt. Meanwhile, a female SHOPPER is chatting with the GROCER.
He's probably halfway to California
If you ask me, he's holing up in someone's house. Waiting for things
to cool down.
I just can't get his face out of my head. The worst part is knowing a man like that has nothing to lose. Life, to a person like that, is worth about ten cents.
Probably has some family tied up in their basement. They'll get him eventually. They always do. Just hopefully before... (notices Henry) Oh, hey Henry, how's your mother?
She's doing great.
The grocer starts to ring Henry up. We look up over Henry's shoulder at him. A child's POV of an adult.
(re: the groceries) Where's all the canned goods? You usually look like you're planning for a tornado.
Yeah, special recipe.
The grocer picks up a MAN'S RAZOR amongst Henry's items.
Henry, this is a man's razor. Your mom wants one of the pink ones on
aisle seven.
It's not for my mom.
The grocer raises an eyebrow.
It's for me.
The grocer examines Henry's face. He looks suspicious.
Practicing? Yeah.
Alright, just do me a favor and keep the plastic cap on, okay?
EXT. MAIN STREET, HOLTON MILLS - DAY

Henry is walking his bike with the grocery bags on the handle bars when he stops in front of a window display for a clothing store. In the display is a family of mannequins on what looks like a camping trip. The father is wearing a peach plaid short sleeved shirt. Henry checks his money.
EXT. SUBURBAN STREET - DAY

Henry walks with the two bags of groceries hanging from his handle bars. A group of kids WHIZ BY ON BIKES. Henry comes upon a stretch of trees, each one stapled with a POLICE WARNING featuring FRANK'S PRISON PHOTO. Henry sees a policeman, OFFICER TREADWELL actually stapling one next to his squad car. He tips his hat to Henry.
Keep an eye out, alright?
Henry nods.
INT. ENTRYWAY, ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
Henry gets the door open with his foot and enters, carrying the groceries. He heads to the kitchen, kicking the door closed with his heel. We follow it until it almost closes, but doesn't. Mom?
No answer. Fear strikes Henry - Did Frank run away with Adele?
INT. KITCHEN, ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
Henry walks in and sets the groceries down. He sees Frank and Adele sitting on the swing on the back porch.
(quietly) I was nineteen years old. (quietly) You were just a boy...
EXT. ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
A STATION WAGON pulls up next to the house. The driver, a woman in her early forties, opens the back passenger door and removes a folded wheelchair. It opens with a "click".
INT. KITCHEN, ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
Henry gets closer to the window.
(quietly) Eighteen years in stir. When I saw that open window, I knew there was a reason to jump.
EXT. ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
The wheelchair is pulled up Adele's front steps, one at a time, landing with a thud on each wooden plank.
INT. KITCHEN, ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
Henry is now standing right next to the window, inches away from Frank and Adele.
Henry shoots a look to the front door, to see EVELYN, walking from the entryway into the living room. There isn't enough time to warn Frank. He runs through the dining room and into the...
INT. LIVING ROOM - DAY
Henry catches Evelyn, his mother's only friend and a demanding harsh one at that.
Oh, hi Henry. Where's your mother?
I don't know...
I need her to watch Barry.
We pivot around Evelyn, to find her son BARRY outside the front door sitting in a wheelchair. We quickly can tell by the way he's moving his arms that he is developmentally disabled. ADELE?
Evelyn goes for the kitchen.
(to Henry) Keep an eye on Barry, would you?
I think my mom's upstairs.
Evelyn stops and switches to the stairs. Henry waits a second for Evelyn to clear the landing, then runs into...
INT. KITCHEN, ADELE'S HOUSE
Henry runs right into FRANK HOLDING A KNIFE to Adele. Or is he holding Adele and the knife is just there. The moment is confusing. He stops Henry and Shhhh's.... him. He pushes Adele out the kitchen.
(a little shaken) Evelyn, is that you?
Henry goes to follow, but Frank grabs him and pulls him back.
(explaining) I said I'd watch Barry.

Frank peaks out the kitchen. He sees a piece of the wheelchair outside the front door. He thinks. Then nods and sends Henry out. As Henry walks up to Barry, we begin hearing Evelyn asking Adele for a favor as they descend the stairs.
It's just not the best timing.
They're not expecting my dad to hold on long.
Normally, I wouldn't think twice...
I don't have any other options, Adele.
Look, I want to help you, but...
I promise he'll be good. Come on, you're not going anywhere...
They're back standing next to Henry. Evelyn begins smoothing down her son's hair.
(to Barry) Do you remember Heeeeenry and his mooooom and all the good times you've had here?
Hey Barry, how's it going?
Barry gurgles back. Syllables lost in saliva.
Please. One mother to another. I wouldn't ask if I wasn't desperate.
A long beat before Adele drops her shoulders. Okay.
Thank you, thank you, thank you. I owe you. (already leaving) I'll be back by the end of the night. It's about two hours drive.

Just put his chair in front of the TV. He loves cartoons. And just like that, she's gone. Henry pulls Barry's chair into the entryway and Adele shuts the door. Frank walks into the room. We're not quite sure what he thinks of all this for a second. And then...
Hey Barry, you look mighty toasty
in all those clothes. Barry is SWEATING in a parka.
(taking off the parka)
I swear, she dresses him for winter
(smiles to Barry) You got here just in time. We needed a third baseman.
EXT. ADELE'S BACKYARD - DAY
We see makeshift bases being laid out. Frank lowers Barry into a LAWN CHAIR, then unbrims his RED SOX HAT from Pricemart and slides it onto Barry's head, who gurgles back with joy.
I'm Frank by the way.
Barry GURGLES back a sound that vaguely resembles FWAANN.
That's right, Frank. Pleasure to meet you.
Frank walks over to Henry who is gloving his new baseball.
Alright, let's see that arm.
Don't get your hopes up.
(not having any of it) Want to run that by me again?
Henry quickly quiets. They take positions around thirty feet away from each other. Frank pauses, then throws it right into Henry's glove with a little heat.
Thing was, when Frank threw the
They continue to throw the ball back and forth.
INT. JERVIS'S HOUSE - SAME

Jervis looks out the window and something catches his eye. From his angle, it appears that Henry is having a game of catch with an unseen person behind the house. He sees Henry in the backyard toss the ball. A moment later it is thrown back, strong and true, by someone out of Jervis's sight-line. He frowns.
EXT. BACKYARD, ADELE'S HOUSE
Frank teaches Henry to hit. He starts by adjusting Henry's stance. Positions his elbow. FLASH IMAGE: ADELE TEACHING HENRY TO DANCE FRANK WINDS UP TO PITCH HENRY A BALL
(whispers) See the ball.
(whispers) See the ball.
Frank throws and Henry connects. Barry hoots. Adele cheers.
There it is. If I had a whole season to really work with you, we could really get somewhere. (turns to Adele) Alright, your turn. No.
Come on. You know I won't take no for an answer.
Frank helps Adele up. He begins positioning her, almost like a dancer. He's behind her, forming his body to hers, in a batter's stance.
Alright Hank, bring us the heat.
Henry takes position. He throws the ball. Frank and Adele swing the bat cracking the ball far. Barry yelps. They continue to play as we hear...
During Frank's two years in Vietnam, he received only one letter from Mandy.
EXT. BUS STATION - DAY
Frank steps off the bus.
It came out of nowhere, just days
before the end of his tour. Mandy, dressed to seduce, runs up and grabs him. There's something coarse about the way she kisses him.
INT. FRANK'S BEDROOM - DAY
Mandy pushes Frank onto the bed. She's down at his feet taking off his boots.
I bet you've been starved for this, right, baby? Or all you got was yellow girls?
Frank is caught up in the momentum but we can tell things are moving a little fast for him. Mandy rides Frank in an uncomfortable bit of love making. She seems on a mission.
There wasn't an exact moment when she asked to be his girlfriend.
INT. FRANK'S BATHROOM - THE NEXT DAY
Mandy is setting up her cosmetics. ADULT HENRY (V.O.) Her clothes. Her underwear. Deodorant, hot rollers, nail kit seemed to all arrive at once.
EXT. FRANK'S GRANDPARENT'S FARM - DAY
Frank is checking the knee high corn. Mandy comes running up. ADULT HENRY (V.O.) A couple weeks later, she came home from the doctor with news.
You must-a-bin storing it all up so long, those sperms of yours were three times as powerful as regular.
Guess our good fortune just came early.
INT. JUDGE'S CHAMBERS - DAY

I'd take twenty more years just to spend another three days with you. As long as I am alive, you are never alone, Adele.

Frank kisses her one more time. It lingers on the end of their lips for a moment, then he quickly stands. We watch from Henry's point of view as Frank steps up to the entryway. He slowly opens the front door. A FLOOD OF SOUND enters the house. Sirens. The Chopper. Shotguns chambering rounds.
(over a bullhorn) HANDS UP! HANDS OVER YOUR HEAD!
Frank complies, raising his palms to the sky.
SERGEANT (O.C.) (CONT'D) (over a bullhorn)
SLOWLY NOW, ONE FOOT AFTER THE Frank steps out of the doorway. Out of our view.
TURN AROUND, FACE THE HOUSE... NOW DOWN ON YOUR KNEES... Adele finally lets go. She's crying. Henry looks lost.
PLACE YOUR HANDS ON YOUR HEAD... INTERLACE YOUR FINGERS... A SUDDEN RUSH OF SOUND as we hear the officers surround Frank and CUFF HIM. Within a moment, OFFICERS storm into the house,
"CLEARING" each room.
Two officers drop down next to Adele and Henry, releasing them from their binds. A PARAMEDIC puts a blanket around Adele. Notices her crying.
Give me room...! (to Adele) Okay, I need to run a couple tests... Can you hear me okay? Tell me your name...
A SPECIAL SERVICE OFFICER kneels next to Henry.
Don't be scared, son. Your mom is going to be fine. We got the guy. He won't be able to do anything to you and your mom anymore.
Henry looks out the window and sees Frank being shoved towards a police car.
EXT. ADELE'S HOUSE - MINUTES LATER
Adele is being walked out. The blanket is still over her shoulders. Henry follows her. Gerald's MINIVAN pulls up outside the POLICE TAPE.
(getting out of the van) Henry...? Henry?!

Just as this is happening, The SQUAD CAR carrying FRANK begins to pull out from the crime scene. Adele and Frank make eye contact. Through the moving reflections along the window, we barely see Frank mouth one word... "Adele..." The squad car slowly passes Gerald, who looks into the backseat incredulous as to what is still going on.
They charged him with kidnapping.
INT. COURT ROOM - DAY
Showing Frank at his sentencing hearing. He's lead back through a doorway into a HOLDING CELL.
This time, they'd lock him up and throw away the key, they said.
EXT. CAPITAL BUILDING - DAY
Adele and Henry enter the gilded front doors.
When my mother heard, she drove to see the prosecutor with me as a witness, to make him understand that no unlawful detainment had taken place.
INT. PROSECUTOR'S OFFICE - DAY
Adele is pouring her heart out for what looks like a hardliner PROSECUTOR.
She had invited Frank into her home. He was good to her son. He was good to her. She omitted the fact that they were in love.
Adele, these situations are never simple. Never clean. (MORE)

May take years for you to fully weave it all together. Perhaps before you put this kind of statement into record, you might want to take a little time to think about what actually happened. Harboring a fugitive is a major crime that we don't look lightly upon. And of course there's delinquency to a minor. You could lose custody of your son...
Adele looks to Henry and back to the prosecutor.
INT. OLD PRISON - DAY
Old steel bars have rusted over. Frank is let out of his cell. He walks up to a pass through window, where he is handed a lunch tray.
They gave Frank ten years for the escape and fifteen for the kidnapping.
Corridor of lunch tables. Frank sits down and eats.
He was designated high-risk and required a maximum security prison.
EXT. MAXIMUM SECURITY PRISON - DAY
Adele's station wagon pulls up to the gate.
INT. WAITING ROOM, PRISON - DAY
Adele is being explained something by a PRISON GUARD.
My mother tried to visit him once, but was told he was doing solitary. Sometime after that, they transferred him to someplace in Massachusetts.
INT. KITCHEN, ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
Adele tries to open a can of soup. Her hands shake worse than we've ever seen them.
The shaking got worse.
Adele gives up on the can.
Within a month, my mother voluntarily relinquished custody of me to my father.
EXT. GERALD'S HOUSE - DAY
Gerald helps Henry with his stuff, carrying it from the minivan up to the house.
INT. RICHARD'S ROOM - DAY
Gerald finishes installing a bunk bed. Richard isn't thrilled.
INT. RICHARD'S ROOM - NIGHT
Henry lies on the top bunk, staring at the ceiling. ADULT HENRY (V.O.) The nights were quiet now. Save for when Chloe woke up with a nightmare. We hear Chloe crying. We hear Marjorie get out of bed.
INT. BREAKFAST ROOM, GERALD'S HOUSE - DAY 254
Henry and Richard eat cereal across from each other. Marjorie makes sure Chloe eats. Gerald stops reading his paper to say something to Henry, but comes up empty.
My Father rarely knew what to say. More often than not, he just kept quiet. The newspapers had said it all anyway.
INT. LOCKER HALLWAY, JUNIOR HIGH - DAY
Henry transfers books from his backpack to his locker.
Seventh grade came with a new and unexpected fame that translated into something like popularity.
We begin to catch everyone around Henry taking notice of him. Rachel McCann stops next to Henry.
Is it true that he tortured you? No.
I heard your mother was his sex slave.
Henry doesn't know how to process this statement.
That's weird.
I just wanted you to know I think you're incredibly brave. If you ever want to talk about it, I'm here for you.
EXT. WOODED AREA BEHIND SCHOOL - LATE AFTERNOON
Henry and Rachel are walking amongst the trees.
Two months later, I got my first kiss all over again.
Henry and Rachel have the sweetest kiss.
INT. LIVING ROOM, ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
Henry steps inside. Adele is making busy. Putting things away. Acting as though she is overwhelmed.
When I visited my mother, she would
act busy and distracted. Calls to
presence made her uncomfortable. A strange reminder of what was missing.
INT. ADELE'S BEDROOM - NIGHT
Adele's night stand is a mountain of books.
The only distraction she had was
her reading. We find Adele in bed reading a book.
In particular, a book called Living
The Good Life, about a couple who
rural Maine to live off of the land.
INT. KITCHEN, ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
Adele and Henry are eating soup. The book is sitting open, face down, half-read for the fiftieth time.
All those two had was each other. That was enough.
EXT. GERALD'S HOUSE - DAY
Henry, now 16 YEARS OLD, comes whistling out of the driveway on a mountain bike, wearing a flannel and cargo pants. ADULT HENRY (V.O.)
brought up the events leading up to seventh grade.
INT. HIGH SCHOOL CORRIDOR - DAY
Henry hangs with friends. He flirts with Rachel, now 16.
First the first time, I had someone to call a girlfriend.
INT. ADELE'S STATION WAGON - NIGHT
Henry and Rachel are driving down the highway on a date. There's a BUMP, and the car begins to drive lop sided. They've run a FLAT TIRE.
EXT. ROAD SIDE - NIGHT
The car is up on a JACK. RACHEL watches as Henry rolls over the spare tire and begins to fix the flat.
Impressive.
Henry controls a proud smile. Henry, wearing a baseball uniform, takes a practice swing on the way to the plate.
Henry lines up at the plate. We hear just under his breath...
See the ball..
Pitch. Swing. Crack! Line drive between left and center.
EXT. VERMONT COUNTRY ROAD - AFTERNOON
It's Fall. The foliage is incredible.
As my senior year began, my father realized if we didn't take some time for the two of us to bond, it would never actually happen.
Over the crest of a hill, Henry and Gerald appear on bicycles wearing road bike cycling gear. We follow them along the rolling hills.
We carved a route along the Connecticut River just as the trees were changing. We stayed at motels and found dives to dine in.
INT. RUN DOWN PRIME RIB JOINT - NIGHT 263
Henry and Gerald eat at a dark wooden table with red gingham table cloths and little yellow candles.
There's something I've been meaning to talk to you about.
Oh, dad, please don't. We're having such a pleasant time.
Gerald laughs it off a little.
Look, this is all I'll say. Everybody talks about all this crazy, wild passion. That's how it goes in the songs. Your mother was like that.
Henry looks up.
She was in love with love. She couldn't do anything partway. Henry has stopped eating. He never gets to hear these things.

That trip we took to California. We had no money. Slept in the car. One town in Nebraska, we got a motel room with a kitchenette. We made spaghetti on a hot plate. She was a terrific woman. Funny. Beautiful.
Gerald smiles. Lost in thought.

I had never thought I looked anything like my father. We seemed so different. But looking across the table then, as he spoke about my mother, I recognized, weirdly, something familiar. I could imagine him being young.
INT. KITCHENETTE, MOTEL - DAY
Adele and Gerald are making spaghetti on that hot plate. ADULT HENRY (V.O.) Just as my mom described. They dance.
Knowing just how much pressure to apply to a woman's back.
He dips her.
The crazy young man she had trusted
to keep her from falling.
INT. RUN DOWN PRIME RIB JOINT - NIGHT
Gerald continues.

A better man might have stayed around to help her through all that sadness. I couldn't bear it. I wanted a regular life. With Marjorie, it wasn't as if I could erase what happened, but it was easier for me, not to think about it. Where for your mother, the story never went away.
Henry and his father make real long lasting eye contact. Both refusing to get closer. Both refusing to cry.
I'm not sure what these last few years have done to repair... but I'm sorry I cut out on you.
A long beat.
Gerald needs to stand up before he loses it.
I'm going to get the check.
The next morning we rode a little more, but the trip had outrun its natural course.
INT. MINIVAN - DAY
They drive in silence down the beautiful Autumn highway.
On the way home, I mostly slept.
INT. HALLWAY, GERALD'S HOUSE - DAY
Henry approaches his father to say something.
The next day, when I suggested I move back in with my mother for the rest
of senior year, he didn't argue. They hug.
EXT. ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
Henry walks his suitcase back up his mom's front steps. He gives her a kiss on the cheek.
That winter I convinced my mom to get
what Marjorie would call a real job.
INT. SENIOR CENTER - DAY
Adele leads a dance class.
The senior center heard about her former dance career and reached out to see if she would lead some of
the old-timers through Fox Trots,
She takes one of the older men in her hands and starts dancing him around the room.
INT. KITCHEN, ADELE'S HOUSE - DAY
Henry prepares dinner for his mother.
It was there in my mom's tiny kitchen, my final summer at home, that I first tried my hand at cooking.
Henry pulls something out of the oven and sets it down in front of Adele. She can't quite believe it. It's the pie. Frank's pie. The smell alone makes her emotional.
INT. KITCHEN, PIE SHOP - DAY
Adult Henry sits frozen, staring at the letter in his hands.
INT. NEW PRISON LIBRARY - DAY
The unmistakable back of Frank's head. Hair now peppered grey. We don't see his face. He's at a desk. Writing.
Dear Henry. I hope you will remember me. We once spent Labor Day weekend together. Five of the best days of my life.
INT. KITCHEN, PIE SHOP - DAY
Henry reads along.
I had always wondered who you would become and what you would look like. And then, one day while flipping through a magazine, I came across a familiar looking pie and there you were.
Henry's eyes begin to moisten.
INT. NEW PRISON LIBRARY - DAY
We now see Frank. Just as handsome. Somehow prison has not destroyed all that is good in the depth of his eyes. The library is so modern, it almost looks like a high school.
The opportunities for me to impart knowledge of any kind have been limited in my life, so it's nice to think I might have contributed some small piece of wisdom or know-how. I'm proud of you.
INT. HENRY'S MASTER BEDROOM - NIGHT
Henry is in bed with his WIFE, a brunette of similar age who has a similar literary quality to her. They read the letter.
I will understand if you choose not to respond to this letter. Your silence would be as much word as I would need, to discontinue any thought of further communication.
INT. FRANK'S CELL, MODERN PRISON - DAY
Frank looks out a thin window on a rainy day. The changes in the prison are enormous. His bed is stainless steel. The window, a long horizontal slit in the wall.

However, I am due to be released shortly. I'm no longer young, but I have plenty of good health remaining, and I'd like to live much the way we discussed that weekend, back when we thought we'd be a family.
Frank hears a knock on his cell door. He passes the sealed envelope to the mail service.
INT. HENRY'S BEDROOM - NIGHT
Henry and his wife continue to read the letter.
Most likely, your mother has remarried by now and is living with a husband somewhere. While this thought haunts me, I'd be relieved to know that she had found happiness. She deserves it.
Henry looks to his wife.
But on the off chance that she might be alone, I wanted to ask you whether I might write her a letter. I promise you, I would sooner cut off my own hand than bring any more grief to Adele.
Henry puts down the letter.
I wrote him back that day to say it wouldn't be difficult to locate my mother. She lived at the same address.
EXT. ADELE'S HOUSE - MODERN DAY
As we push in, we notice that the house has deteriorated.
And it would be far less difficult to locate his place in her heart.
EXT. MODERN PRISON - MODERN DAY A parking lot and a fence.
ADULT HENRY (V.O.)
We see Adele now in her late fifties. She leans against her car wearing a coat, waiting for the fence to open.
The real drug, I came to believe, was love.
The fence begins to move. It reveals Frank. Adele sits up. They see each other for the first time in nearly twenty years. It's as emotional as you'd imagine.
EXT. COUNTRY ROAD - MODERN DAY
We see Frank and Adele driving along a country road. Everything is quiet and serene. ADULT HENRY (V.O.)
They look at each other, checking to make sure they're actually sitting in the same car. She holds his arm.
They were two people who could not go out into the world, so they made a world with each other.
EXT. FRANK'S FARM - LATE AFTERNOON
Henry and his wife find the old dirt driveway that leads up to Frank's grandmother's farm. They turn and start up the bumpy road.
INT. HENRY'S CAR - SAME
Henry takes in the scene. This is obviously his first time visiting the farm.
EXT. FARM HOUSE - LATE AFTERNOON 287
Henry steps out of the car. His wife opens the rear passenger door and picks up their INFANT out of the carseat. Henry looks up at the farm house. His mother and Frank nowhere to be seen.
EXT. FIELD BEHIND THE FARM HOUSE - LATE AFTERNOON 288

Henry and his wife come around the back side of the farm house. Looking down past the field where we once saw Frank and Mandy run as teenagers, we see a small garden. In the late afternoon light, we can see Frank and Adele picking vegetables. Perhaps even a few roaming chickens. After a beat, they look back up and wave. Henry smiles as he leads his family down to the garden. Perhaps rolling up his sleeves. Adele notices her son approaching and stops.
(almost a whisper) You brought the baby.
